subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Amongst the following, the strongest base in aqueous medium is ________.
question_html: Amongst the following, the strongest base in aqueous medium is ________.

options.explanation: Greater is the stability of the substituted ammonium cation, stronger should be the corresponding amine as a base. Thus, the order of the basicity of aliphatic amines should be: primary > secondary > tertiary, which is opposite to the inductive effect based order. Further C 6 H 5 NHCH 3 is less basic than both CH 3 NH 2 and (CH 3 ) 2 NH due to the delocalization of the lone pair of electrons present on the nitrogen atom into the benzene ring.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following is the weakest Brönsted base?
question_html: Which of the following is the weakest Brönsted base?

options.explanation: Due to the delocalization of the lone pair of electrons on the N-atom into the benzene ring making it less available for protonation, C 6 H 5 NH 2 is the weakest base. Resonating Structure of Aniline

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Benzylamine may be alkylated as shown in the following equation: C 6 H 5 CH 2 NH 2 + R-X {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 6 H 5 CH 2 NHR Which of the following alkyl halides is best suited for this reaction through S N 1 mechanism?
question_html:
Benzylamine may be alkylated as shown in the following equation:
C6H5CH2NH2 + R-X {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C6H5CH2NHR
Which of the following alkyl halides is best suited for this reaction through SN1 mechanism?

options.explanation: SN1 substitution reaction occurs in two steps. In the first step, R-X bond is broken to produce a carbocation that is attacked by the nucleophile. The greater the stability of carbocation, the greater will be the rate of reaction. Benzylic halides show high reactivity towards S N 1 reaction.

question_html: Which of the following compound will not undergo azo coupling reaction with benzene diazonium chloride.

options.explanation: Diazonium cation is a weak electrophile and hence reacts with electron-rich compounds containing electron-donating groups such as -OH, -NH, and -OCH groups and not with compounds containing electron-withdrawing groups such as -NO 2 , etc. Hence not react with nitrobenzene.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Among the following amines, the strongest Brönsted base is ________.
question_html: Among the following amines, the strongest Brönsted base is ________.

options.explanation: Aniline is a weaker base than NH 3 due to the delocalization of the lone pair of electrons on the N-atom into the benzene ring and less available for protonation. Pyrrole is not at all basic because the lone pair of electrons on N -atom is donated towards aromatic sextet formation. Therefore, pyrrolidine has a strong tendency to accept a proton and hence the strongest base.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The correct decreasing order of basic strength of the following species is ________. H 2 O, NH 3 , OH - , {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{2}^-{/tex}
question_html:
The correct decreasing order of basic strength of the following species is ________.
H2O, NH3, OH-, {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{2}^-{/tex}

options.explanation: {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{2}^-{/tex} > OH > NH 3 > H 2 O. Due to higher electronegativity of O than N atom, the O-H bond is more polar than the N-H bond. Hence, O-H is more acidic in nature than the N-H bond. Now, NH 2 and OH have a negative charge due to which they are more basic than NH 3 and H 2 O.

question_text: Which of the following methods of preparation of amines will give same number of carbon atoms in the chain of amines as in the reactant?
question_html: Which of the following methods of preparation of amines will give same number of carbon atoms in the chain of amines as in the reactant?

options.text: Reaction of amide with LiAlH 4 followed by treatment with water
options.html: Reaction of amide with LiAlH4 followed by treatment with water

options.text: Heating alkylhalide with potassium salt of phthalimide followed by hydrolysis
options.html: Heating alkylhalide with potassium salt of phthalimide followed by hydrolysis

options.text: Treatment of amide with bromine in an aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide
options.html: Treatment of amide with bromine in an aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide
options.explanation: Aliphatic and aryl/alkyl primary amines can be prepared by the reduction of the corresponding nitriles with lithium aluminium hydride LiAIH 4 . {tex}\mathop {{\text{R}} - {\text{C}} \equiv {\text{N}}}\limits_{Alkynitrite} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{Ar}} - {\text{C}} \equiv {\text{N}} \to {\text{LiAl}}{{\text{H}}_4}}\limits_{Arynitirite} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{RC}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2}}\limits_{{1^o}a\min e} {/tex} Heating alkyl halide with primary, secondary, and tertiary amine can be prepared by reduction of LiAlH 4 ether followed by treatment with water. {tex}{\text{R}} - {\text{CON}}{{\text{H}}_2}\xrightarrow[{(ii){H_2}O}]{{(i)LiAl{H_4}/ether}}{\text{R}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2}{/tex} Heating alkyl halide with potassium salt of phthalimide followed by hydrolysis produces primary amine. This process is known as Gabriel's phthalimide reaction. The number of carbon atoms in the chain of amines of the product is the same as a reactant.

correct_option_text: Treatment of amide with bromine in an aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide

question_text: Which of the following cannot be prepared by Sandmeyer’s reaction? Chlorobenzene Bromobenzene Iodobenzene Fluorobenzene
question_html:
Which of the following cannot be prepared by Sandmeyer’s reaction?

  1. Chlorobenzene
  2. Bromobenzene
  3. Iodobenzene
  4. Fluorobenzene
solution_text: The correct options will be (iii) and (iv) because chloro and Bromo arenes are easily prepared by Sandmeyer's reaction. lodoarenes are prepared by simply warming the diazonium salt solution with an aqueous KI solution. Fluoroarenes are prepared by Balz-Schiemann's reaction. All other reagents give aniline.
solution_html: The correct options will be (iii) and (iv) because chloro and Bromo arenes are easily prepared by Sandmeyer's reaction. lodoarenes are prepared by simply warming the diazonium salt solution with an aqueous KI solution. Fluoroarenes are prepared by Balz-Schiemann's reaction. All other reagents give aniline.

question_text: Which of the following species are involved in the carbylamine test? R-NC CHCl 3 COCl 2 NaNO 2 + HCl
question_html:
Which of the following species are involved in the carbylamine test?

  1. R-NC
  2. CHCl3
  3. COCl2
  4. NaNO2 + HCl
solution_text: (i) and (ii) are the correct option because of Carbylamine reaction. Amine on heating with a mixture of chloroform CHCl 3 and KOH produces alkyl isocyanate. R-NH 2 + CHCl 3 + 3KOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} RNC+ 3KCl + 3H 2 O Only RNC and CHCl 3 are involved in carbylamine reaction.
solution_html:
(i) and (ii) are the correct option because of Carbylamine reaction.
Amine on heating with a mixture of chloroform CHCl3 and KOH produces alkyl isocyanate.
R-NH2 + CHCl3 + 3KOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} RNC+ 3KCl + 3H2O Only RNC and CHCl3 are involved in carbylamine reaction.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following amines can be prepared by Gabriel synthesis. Isobutyl amine 2-Phenylethylamine N-methylbenzylamine Aniline
question_html:
Which of the following amines can be prepared by Gabriel synthesis.

  1. Isobutyl amine
  2. 2-Phenylethylamine
  3. N-methylbenzylamine
  4. Aniline
solution_text: Only primary aliphatic amines such as (i) (CH 3 ) 2 CH-CH 2 NH 2 (ii) C 6 H5CH 2 NH 2 can be prepared by Gabriel synthesis. 2 o amines, i.e. C 6 H 5 CH 2 NHCH 3 (iii) and 1 o amine, C 6 H 5 NH 2 (iv), however, cannot be prepared by Gabriel synthesis.
solution_html: Only primary aliphatic amines such as (i) (CH3)2 CH-CH2 NH2 (ii) C6H5CH2NH2 can be prepared by Gabriel synthesis. 2o amines, i.e. C6H5CH2NHCH3 (iii) and 1o amine, C6H5NH2 (iv), however, cannot be prepared by Gabriel synthesis.

book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Under which of the following reaction conditions, aniline gives p-nitro derivative as the major product? Acetyl chloride/pyridine followed by reaction with conc. H 2 SO 4 + conc. HNO 3 . Acetic anyhdride/pyridine followed by conc. H 2 SO 4 + conc. HNO 3 . Dil. HCl followed by reaction with conc. H 2 SO 4 + conc. HNO 3 . Reaction with conc. HNO 3 + conc.H 2 SO 4 .
question_html:
Under which of the following reaction conditions, aniline gives p-nitro derivative as the major product?

  1. Acetyl chloride/pyridine followed by reaction with conc. H2SO4 + conc. HNO3.
  2. Acetic anyhdride/pyridine followed by conc. H2SO4 + conc. HNO3.
  3. Dil. HCl followed by reaction with conc. H2SO4 + conc. HNO3.
  4. Reaction with conc. HNO3 + conc.H2SO4.
solution_text: (i), (ii) are the correct options because of Aniline or reaction with acetyl chloride or acetic anhydride in the presence of pyridine produces N-acetyl aniline which is an ortho, para directing group which on further reaction with the nitrating mixture (conc. HNO 3 + conc. H 2 SO 4 ) produces p-nitroaniline as the major product preferentially as shown below:
solution_html:
(i), (ii) are the correct options because of Aniline or reaction with acetyl chloride or acetic anhydride in the presence of
pyridine produces N-acetyl aniline which is an ortho, para directing group which on further reaction with the nitrating mixture (conc. HNO3 + conc. H2SO4) produces p-nitroaniline as the major product preferentially as shown below:

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following reactions belong to electrophilic aromatic substitution? Bromination of acetanilide Coupling reaction of aryldiazonium salts Diazotisation of aniline Acylation of aniline
question_html:
Which of the following reactions belong to electrophilic aromatic substitution?

  1. Bromination of acetanilide
  2. Coupling reaction of aryldiazonium salts
  3. Diazotisation of aniline
  4. Acylation of aniline
solution_text: (i) and (ii) are the correct options because Acylation is a nucleophilic substitution reaction in which H atom of -NH 2 is replaced by an acyl group. Diazotisation is also a nucleophilic substitution reaction.
solution_html: (i) and (ii) are the correct options because Acylation is a nucleophilic substitution reaction in which H atom of -NH2 is replaced by an acyl group. Diazotisation is also a nucleophilic substitution reaction.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: What is the role of HNO 3 in the nitrating mixture used for nitration of benzene?
question_html: What is the role of HNO3 in the nitrating mixture used for nitration of benzene?
solution_text: HNO 3 acts as a base in the nitrating mixture and provides the electrophile, {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{+}{/tex} (nitronium ion).
solution_html: HNO3 acts as a base in the nitrating mixture and provides the electrophile, {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{+}{/tex} (nitronium ion).

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why is NH 2 group of aniline acetylated before carrying out nitration?
question_html: Why is NH2 group of aniline acetylated before carrying out nitration?
solution_text: Direct nitration of aniline is not possible on account of oxidation of the NH 2 group to yeild tarry oxidation products in addition to nitro derivatives. However, nitration can be carried after protecting the -NH 2 group by acetylation to give acetanilide which is then nitrated and finally hydrolyzed to give o- and p-nitroanilines. The acetyl group being electron-withdrawing attracts the lone pair of electrons of the N-atom towards the carbonyl group. As a result, the activating effect -NH 2 group is reduced i.e., the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen is less available for donation to the benzene ring by resonance. Therefore, the activating effect of -NHCOCH 3 group is less than that of -NH 2 group.
solution_html:
Direct nitration of aniline is not possible on account of oxidation of the NH2 group to yeild tarry oxidation products in addition to nitro derivatives. However, nitration can be carried after protecting the -NH2 group by acetylation to give acetanilide which is then nitrated and finally hydrolyzed to give o- and p-nitroanilines. The acetyl group being electron-withdrawing attracts the lone pair of electrons of the N-atom towards the carbonyl group.

As a result, the activating effect -NH2 group is reduced i.e., the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen is less available for donation to the benzene ring by resonance. Therefore, the activating effect of -NHCOCH3 group is less than that of -NH2 group.

question_text: Explain why MeNH 2 is a stronger base than MeOH?
question_html: Explain why MeNH2 is a stronger base than MeOH?
solution_text: Nitrogen (N) is less electronegative than oxygen (O), therefore, the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen is readily available for donation. Hence, MeNH 2 is more basic than MeOH.
solution_html: Nitrogen (N) is less electronegative than oxygen (O), therefore, the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen is readily available for donation. Hence, MeNH2 is more basic than MeOH.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines?
question_html: What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines?
solution_text: Pyridine, (CH 3 CO) 2 O and other bases are used to remove the side product i.e., HCl formed during reaction mixture.
solution_html: Pyridine, (CH3CO)2O and other bases are used to remove the side product i.e., HCl formed during reaction mixture.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Under what reaction conditions (acidic/basic), the coupling reaction of aryldiazonium chloride with aniline is carried out?
question_html: Under what reaction conditions (acidic/basic), the coupling reaction of aryldiazonium chloride with aniline is carried out?
solution_text: The coupling reaction of aryl diazonium chloride with aniline is carried out in mild acidic condition i.e. pH = 4 - 5.
solution_html: The coupling reaction of aryl diazonium chloride with aniline is carried out in mild acidic condition i.e. pH = 4 - 5.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Predict the product of reaction of aniline with bromine in non-polar solvent such as CS 2 .
question_html: Predict the product of reaction of aniline with bromine in non-polar solvent such as CS2.
solution_text: In non-polar solvent (such as CS 2 ) the activating effect of -NH 2 group is reduced (due to resonance) and hence, mono substitution occurs only at o- and p-positions while para is a minor product and ortho is a major product.
solution_html: In non-polar solvent (such as CS2) the activating effect of -NH2 group is reduced (due to resonance) and hence, mono substitution occurs only at o- and p-positions while para is a minor product and ortho is a major product.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of dipole moment. CH 3 CH 2 CH 3 , CH 3 CH 2 NH 2 , CH 3 CH 2 OH
question_html:
Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of dipole moment.
CH3CH2CH3, CH3CH2NH2, CH3CH2OH
solution_text: CH 3 CH 2 CH 3 < CH 3 CH 2 NH 2 < CH 3 CH 2 OH Since 0 is more electronegative than N, therefore, the dipole moment of ethyl alcohol is higher than that of ethylamine. Propane, however, has the least dipole moment since it is almost a non-polar molecule.
solution_html:
CH3CH2CH3 < CH3CH2NH2 < CH3CH2OH
Since 0 is more electronegative than N, therefore, the dipole moment of ethyl alcohol is higher than that of ethylamine. Propane, however, has the least dipole moment since it is almost a non-polar molecule.

question_text: What is the structure and IUPAC name of the compound, allylamine?
question_html: What is the structure and IUPAC name of the compound, allylamine?
solution_text: The structure of allylamine is {tex}{{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^3 = \mathop {\text{C}}\limits^2 {\text{H}} - \mathop {\text{C}}\limits^1 {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2}{/tex} and this compound's IUPAC name is prop-2-en-1-amine.
solution_html: The structure of allylamine is {tex}{{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^3 = \mathop {\text{C}}\limits^2 {\text{H}} - \mathop {\text{C}}\limits^1 {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2}{/tex} and this compound's IUPAC name is prop-2-en-1-amine.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A compound Z with molecular formula C 3 H 9 N reacts with C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl to give a solid, insoluble in alkali. Identify Z.
question_html: A compound Z with molecular formula C3H9N reacts with C6H5SO2Cl to give a solid, insoluble in alkali. Identify Z.
solution_text: A compound 'Z' with the molecular formula C 3 H 9 N is which on treatment with C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl (hinsberg's reagent) gives a solid, insoluble in alkali(NaOH), Therefore, the product does not have any replaceable hydrogen on the nitrogen atom. In other words, the amines (Z) must be secondary amine, i.e. Z is ethyl methylamine (C 2 H 5 NHCH 3 ).
solution_html: A compound 'Z' with the molecular formula C3H9N is which on treatment with C6H5SO2Cl (hinsberg's reagent) gives a solid, insoluble in alkali(NaOH), Therefore, the product does not have any replaceable hydrogen on the nitrogen atom. In other words, the amines (Z) must be secondary amine, i.e. Z is ethyl methylamine (C2H5NHCH3).

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A primary amine, RNH 2 can be reacted with CH 3 -X to get secondary amine, R-NHCH 3 but the only disadvantage is that 3° amine and quaternary ammonium salts are also obtained as side products. Can you suggest a method where RNH 2 forms only 2 o amine?
question_html: A primary amine, RNH2 can be reacted with CH3-X to get secondary amine, R-NHCH3 but the only disadvantage is that 3° amine and quaternary ammonium salts are also obtained as side products. Can you suggest a method where RNH2 forms only 2o amine?
solution_text: {tex}\mathop {R - {\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2}}\limits_{{1^o}A\min e} + {\text{CHC}}{{\text{l}}_3} + 3{\text{KOH}} \to \mathop {R - {\text{N}}C}\limits_{Alkyl\,isocyanide} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{(\operatorname{Re} duction)}]{{{H_2}/Pd}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {R - {\text{N}}H - C{H_3}}\limits_{{1^o}A\min e} {/tex} Carbylamine reaction is shown by 1 o amine only which results in the replacement of two hydrogen atoms attached to N atom of -NH 2 group by one carbon atom in heating with choloform to form isocyanide or carbylamine. On catalytic reduction, the isocyanide will give a secondary amine with one methyl group.
solution_html:
{tex}\mathop {R - {\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2}}\limits_{{1^o}A\min e} + {\text{CHC}}{{\text{l}}_3} + 3{\text{KOH}} \to \mathop {R - {\text{N}}C}\limits_{Alkyl\,isocyanide} {/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow[{(\operatorname{Re} duction)}]{{{H_2}/Pd}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {R - {\text{N}}H - C{H_3}}\limits_{{1^o}A\min e} {/tex}
Carbylamine reaction is shown by 1o amine only which results in the replacement of two hydrogen atoms attached to N atom of -NH2 group by one carbon atom in heating with choloform to form isocyanide or carbylamine. On catalytic reduction, the isocyanide will give a secondary amine with one methyl group.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A solution contains 1 g mol. each of p-toluene diazonium chloride and p-nitrophenyl diazonium chloride. To this 1gmol of an alkaline solution of phenol is added. Predict the major product. Explain your answer.
question_html: A solution contains 1 g mol. each of p-toluene diazonium chloride and p-nitrophenyl diazonium chloride. To this 1gmol of an alkaline solution of phenol is added. Predict the major product. Explain your answer.
solution_text: This reaction is an example of electrophilic aromatic substitution(atom attach to aromatic is replaced by an electrophile). In an alkaline medium, phenol forms phenoxide ion which is more electron-rich than phenol and hence more reactive for the electrophilic attack. The electrophile in this reaction is aryl diazonium cation a p-Nitrophenyldiazonium cation (O 2 N-C 6 H 4 -N=N) is a stronger electrophile than p-toluene diazonium cation. Therefore, it couples preferentially with phenol.
solution_html: This reaction is an example of electrophilic aromatic substitution(atom attach to aromatic is replaced by an electrophile). In an alkaline medium, phenol forms phenoxide ion which is more electron-rich than phenol and hence more reactive for the electrophilic attack. The electrophile in this reaction is aryl diazonium cation a p-Nitrophenyldiazonium cation (O2N-C6H4-N=N) is a stronger electrophile than p-toluene diazonium cation. Therefore, it couples preferentially with phenol.

question_text: Match the reactions given in Column I with the statements given in Column II. Column I Column II (a) Ammonolysis (i) Amine with lesser number of carbon atoms (b) Gabriel phthalimide synthesis (ii) Detection test for primary amines. (c) Hoffmann Bromamide reaction (iii) Reaction of phthalimide with KOH and R-X (d) Carbylamine reaction (iv) Reaction of alkylhalides with NH 3
question_html:
Match the reactions given in Column I with the statements given in Column II.

Column I Column II
(a) Ammonolysis (i) Amine with lesser number of carbon atoms
(b) Gabriel phthalimide synthesis (ii) Detection test for primary amines.
(c) Hoffmann Bromamide reaction (iii) Reaction of phthalimide with KOH and R-X
(d) Carbylamine reaction (iv) Reaction of alkylhalides with NH3
solution_text: The correct order of match is (a) - (iv), (b) - (iii), (c) - (i), (d) - (ii).
solution_html: The correct order of match is (a) - (iv), (b) - (iii), (c) - (i), (d) - (ii).

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Assertion (A): Acylation of amines gives a monosubstituted product whereas alkylation of amines gives the polysubstituted product. Reason (R): Acyl group sterically hinders the approach of further acyl groups.
question_html:
Assertion (A): Acylation of amines gives a monosubstituted product whereas alkylation of amines gives the polysubstituted product.
Reason (R): Acyl group sterically hinders the approach of further acyl groups.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.
options.explanation: In alkylation, an amine can react with alkyl halide to form next higher class of amine caused by the presence of electron pair on nitrogen which makes amine to behave as nucleophile and alkyl halide thus undergo nucleophilic substitution reaction. Whereas primary and secondary amines react with acid chlorides, anhydrides and esters they give monosubstituted amides as products. Due to involvement of lone pairs of nitrogen in delocalization within the carbonyl causes decrease in charge density to act as a nucleophile. Hence, acylation gives more substituted products.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

correct_option_text: A is true but R is false.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A hydrocarbon ‘A’, (C 4 H 8 ) on reaction with HCl gives a compound ‘B’, (C 4 H 9 Cl), which on reaction with 1 mol of NH 3 gives compound ‘C’, (C 4 H 11 N). On reacting with NaNO 2 and HCl followed by treatment with water, compound ‘C’ yields optically active alcohol, ‘D’. Ozonolysis of ‘A’ gives 2 mols of acetaldehyde. Identify compounds ‘A’ to ‘D’. Explain the reactions involved.
question_html: A hydrocarbon ‘A’, (C4H8) on reaction with HCl gives a compound ‘B’, (C4H9Cl), which on reaction with 1 mol of NH 3 gives compound ‘C’, (C4H11N). On reacting with NaNO2 and HCl followed by treatment with water, compound ‘C’ yields optically active alcohol, ‘D’. Ozonolysis of ‘A’ gives 2 mols of acetaldehyde. Identify compounds ‘A’ to ‘D’. Explain the reactions involved.
solution_text: Addition of HCl to compound 'A' shows that compound 'A' is an alkene. Compound 'B' is C 4 H 9 Cl. Compound 'B' reacts with NH2, it forms amine 'C'. compound C is C 4 H 9 NH 2 {tex}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_4}{{\text{H}}_8}}\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{HCl}}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_4}{{\text{H}}_9}{\text{Cl}}}\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}}}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_4}{{\text{H}}_{11}}{\text{N}}}\limits_{(C)} {/tex} or C 4 H 9 NH 2 'C' gives diazonium salt with NaNO 2 /HCl, which yields optically active alcohol D(Butan-2-ol). So, 'C' is an aliphatic amine. 'A' on ozonolysis produces 2 moles of CH 3 CHO. So, ' A ' is CH 3 -CH=CH-CH 3 (But-2-ene) Reactions: {tex}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{CH}} = {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{But - 2 - ene({C_4}{H_8})} }\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{HCl}}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_| }\limits_{Cl} {{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{2 - Chlorobu\tan e({C_4}{H_9}Cl)} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}Cl - {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{2 - Chlorobu\tan e} }\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{N{H_3}}}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_| }\limits_{N{H_2}} {{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{2 - A\min o\,bu\tan e({C_4}{H_{11}}N)} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex} {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}\mathop {\xrightarrow{{{\text{NaN}}{{\text{O}}_2}/{\text{HCl}}}}}\limits_{{H_2}O} \mathop {\mathop {CH3 - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{OH}^{C{H_2}C{H_3}} - H}\limits_{{\text{Butan - 2 - ol (Optically active)}}} }\limits_{(D)} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Addition of HCl to compound 'A' shows that compound 'A' is an alkene. Compound 'B' is C4H9Cl.
  2. Compound 'B' reacts with NH2, it forms amine 'C'. compound C is C4H9NH2
    {tex}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_4}{{\text{H}}_8}}\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{HCl}}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_4}{{\text{H}}_9}{\text{Cl}}}\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}}}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_4}{{\text{H}}_{11}}{\text{N}}}\limits_{(C)} {/tex} or C4H9NH2
  3. 'C' gives diazonium salt with NaNO2/HCl, which yields optically active alcohol D(Butan-2-ol). So, 'C' is an aliphatic amine.
  4. 'A' on ozonolysis produces 2 moles of CH3CHO. So, ' A ' is CH3-CH=CH-CH3(But-2-ene)

Reactions: 
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{CH}} = {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{But - 2 - ene({C_4}{H_8})} }\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{HCl}}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_| }\limits_{Cl} {{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{2 - Chlorobu\tan e({C_4}{H_9}Cl)} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}Cl - {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{2 - Chlorobu\tan e} }\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{N{H_3}}}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_| }\limits_{N{H_2}} {{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{2 - A\min o\,bu\tan e({C_4}{H_{11}}N)} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex}
{tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}\mathop {\xrightarrow{{{\text{NaN}}{{\text{O}}_2}/{\text{HCl}}}}}\limits_{{H_2}O} \mathop {\mathop {CH3 - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{OH}^{C{H_2}C{H_3}} - H}\limits_{{\text{Butan - 2 - ol (Optically active)}}} }\limits_{(D)} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A colorless substance ‘A’ (C 6 H 7 N) is sparingly soluble in water and gives a water-soluble compound ‘B’ on treating with a mineral acid. On reacting with CHCl 3 and alcoholic potash ‘A’ produces an obnoxious smell due to the formation of compound ‘C’. The reaction of ‘A’ with benzene sulphonyl chloride gives compound ‘D’ which is soluble in alkali. With NaNO 2 and HCl, ‘A’ forms compound ‘E’ which reacts with phenol in alkaline medium to give an orange dye ‘F’. Identify compounds ‘A’ to ‘F’.
question_html: A colorless substance ‘A’ (C6H7N) is sparingly soluble in water and gives a water-soluble compound ‘B’ on treating with a mineral acid. On reacting with CHCl 3 and alcoholic potash ‘A’ produces an obnoxious smell due to the formation of compound ‘C’. The reaction of ‘A’ with benzene sulphonyl chloride gives compound ‘D’ which is soluble in alkali. With NaNO2 and HCl, ‘A’ forms compound ‘E’ which reacts with phenol in alkaline medium to give an orange dye ‘F’. Identify compounds ‘A’ to ‘F’.
solution_text: Substance 'A'-Aniline, 'B'-Anilinium chloride, 'C'-Benzene isonitrile, 'D'- N-phenylbenzene sulphonamide, 'E'-benzene diazonium chloride, 'F'-p- hydroxyazobenzene.
solution_html: Substance 'A'-Aniline, 'B'-Anilinium chloride, 'C'-Benzene isonitrile, 'D'- N-phenylbenzene sulphonamide, 'E'-benzene diazonium chloride, 'F'-p- hydroxyazobenzene.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Predict the reagent or the product in the following reaction sequence.
question_html: Predict the reagent or the product in the following reaction sequence.
solution_text: Sn/HCl or Fe / HCl, H 2 /Pd OH - /H + H 3 PO 2 /H 2 O
solution_html:
1. Sn/HCl or Fe / HCl, H2/Pd
  3. OH-/H+
  5. H3PO2/H2O
